artifacts.content:
Hi Tere,

I finally have a quiet half hour – kids are in their rooms pretending to do homework and Carlos is passed out on the couch with the Cavs game on.

I keep thinking about how much of this Lions van craziness is really just our family’s medical novela playing out in public. It really started back in March 2023 when I was at Riverbend Community Health Center going through the high‑risk diabetes registry and saw Mami’s name pop up, flagged for uncontrolled Type 2. Then I opened the EMR and there was Dr. Patel’s note from Riverbend Vision Associates: early diabetic retinopathy. That same week she almost fell on the steps outside St. Mark’s Parish Hall because she couldn’t see the curb. I drove her straight from church to the clinic and just sat in the car afterward and cried.

And then there’s Miguel. Remember October 7, 2022, when he was here and we did that little Saturday pilot screening behind Lincoln Elementary “for practice”? His fasting blood sugar over 260 mg/dL, the rush to get him into MetroHealth on Monday for neuropathy work‑up… plus Javi’s pre‑diabetes from the November 2022 RCHC screening, and his A1C 9.4% at the St. Mary’s clinic on August 28, 2021 after he lost his insurance in Toledo. Our whole family is like a walking case study.

All of that is why I built the combined mobile vision/diabetes model and that family‑risk algorithm in January 2023 that flags households like ours in 44128 so they get priority slots when we park at Lincoln or Riverbend Middle.

Anyway, enough health talk. How are classes going for Miguel this semester? Tell him his tía expects him to text me his next A1C, no excuses.

Besos,
Maria

metadata.subject: Why this work feels so personal

artifacts.content:
Personal reflection – late night, kitchen table

I need to remember why I’m so obsessed with the tiny details of this mobile clinic.

1) Family risk patterns. Miguel’s DKA hospitalization at Mercy Hospital Chicago in October 2022, Javi’s pre‑diabetes diagnosis at the November 2022 RCHC screening, then Adrian failing his basic vision screening at Lincoln Elementary in September 2021 with “academic concern/possible learning issue” scribbled in his file before anyone checked his eyes. ClearView Optometry finally naming the strong astigmatism in his right eye. That’s why I built the January 2023 scoring system that quietly flags families in 44128 with 2+ high A1Cs or diabetes ER visits so we can pull them into the Vision & Wellness van days at Lincoln and Riverbend Middle.

2) My own health. May 2024 urgent‑care visit at Riverbend Urgent Care: BP 168/102. Provider basically said, “You can’t keep doing 10–12 extra volunteer hours on top of your full‑time job and expect your heart to like it.” That’s what pushed me to ask Dr. Karen Lewis for the adjusted schedule and to recruit two co‑chairs so the project doesn’t live only in my head.

3) Mental health lens. March 2015, postpartum depression after Adrian’s birth, all those appointments with Dr. Nguyen at Riverbend Behavioral Health Clinic where even fluorescent lights felt like too much. That’s why the mobile van has the quiet corner, low generator noise, and why we did the August 2023 trauma‑informed training at the Lions clubhouse.

Remember: this is about keeping other families from falling through the same cracks we did.

metadata.title: Why the details matter for the van

artifacts.content:
Hi Javi,

Catching up while I wait in the parking lot at Riverbend Community Health Center for Mami’s labs.

I know I sound like a broken record nagging you about screenings, but look at the timeline: you lost your employer insurance in August 2021 after that accident citation out of the Toledo depot, we dragged you to the Lions weekend clinic behind St. Mary’s Church on August 28, 2021 “just to check,” and your A1C came back 9.4%. Then the optometrist from Lakeside Vision saw early diabetic changes you’d been ignoring because of the bills. Fast‑forward a year and Miguel’s DKA at Mercy in October 2022. Our genes are not playing.

Those two cases are literally baked into how I run the van now. The intake algorithm I wrote in January 2023 quietly prioritizes households in 44128 where two or more relatives have elevated A1C or diabetes‑related ER visits. When we park at Lincoln Elementary or Riverbend Middle School, those families get the first call‑backs.

On top of that, watching Mami get briefly turned away in March 2023 at RCHC because her Medicaid renewal was “pending” and they wanted $245 up front for a follow‑up eye exam… I was standing there as the community health program coordinator and her daughter, trying not to explode at the front desk. That’s why our mobile clinic volunteers now walk people through Medicaid and charity‑care options step by step.

Anyway, how are you feeling on the road? Please don’t skip meds to save money. If things get tight, tell me. You know I’ve already done crazier things, like setting up that quiet hardship fund from the $1,200 Carlos and I freed up after paying off the kids’ after‑school fees.

Te quiero,
Maria

metadata.subject: Why I keep bugging you about your numbers

artifacts.content:
Event: Mobile Vision & Wellness Clinic – Riverbend Public Library
Date: Sunday, December 3, 2023
Time: 9:00 a.m. – 3:30 p.m.
Location: Riverbend Public Library parking lot
Attendees: Maria, Lions volunteers (Ana M., Bob H., David T., etc.), RCHC nurse, transit rep

Notes for myself:

– Bring extra BP cuffs and glucometer strips; last time at the library we ran out by 1 p.m.
– Coordinate with Riverbend Transit Authority rep about distributing 20–30 bus passes, especially for folks from Glenwood Avenue apartments and Maple Grove Court.
– Quietly check in on Roberto, the 49‑year‑old custodian from Riverbend Unified who told me in Spanish he’d skipped his blood pressure meds for three months to pay off that $600 payday loan. His reading on 12/3 last year was scary; make sure he gets a same‑week RCHC appointment.
– Keep an eye on Bob Hanley from our club if he comes through again. January 2022 at the Riverbend Senior Center screening he nearly failed the vision test, then admitted to me and the treasurer he’d skipped insulin for three months over the $380 past‑due at Riverbend Pharmacy. We used discretionary funds and a private donation to clear it and set up a payment plan with ClearView for his retinal exam. Still needs gentle nudges, not public shaming.
– Remember: this is also where we caught that data‑exposure issue in late 2022 with the paper intake forms. Double‑check that we’re using the updated LCI‑aligned consent and that all completed forms go straight into the locked bin for scanning.
– Ask library staff if we can set up the small education corner inside by the children’s section again; families linger there longer.

Hi Alan,

I’m working on the draft language for the next Healthy Riverbend Coalition report and wanted to clarify how I’m handling data.

Every quarter I export a de‑identified spreadsheet from our Lions screening roster for Riverbend Community Health Center – clinic ID, age range, zip (mostly 44128 and 44130), service type, referral outcome. That’s what goes to RCHC and sits in the shared folder you and Dr. Kline can see.

Separate from that, I keep a password‑protected master Excel file on my home laptop here at 214 Oakview Terrace. That one links the clinic IDs back to real names, addresses (Oakview, Maple Court, the Glenwood Ave complex, etc.), and insurance status. I use it only to personally call uninsured or under‑insured participants about sliding‑scale enrollment or bus passes. Per your January 2023 advice when we wrote the van refurbishment grant, I don’t email that file or upload it anywhere.

Speaking of the grant, thank you again for helping me finesse the budget language. Showing that the remaining $2,800 for the van came from the RCHC outreach line item we freed up (after Lincoln Elementary pulled the plug on the school‑based screening because of last year’s undocumented student disclosure mess) made the district‑level LCI grant look much cleaner.

On the LCI side, I’m still reconciling our MyLion entries with the internal RCHC exports Daniel Ruiz sends me – names, DOB, insurance status, etc. I run those spreadsheets locally, update the service counts in MyLion, then delete the working files once the month is closed. I’ve also updated our consent forms with the LCI liability language Denise and Lion Patricia had me add after the late‑2022 HIPAA corrective‑action plan.

Let me know if there’s anything in this workflow that worries you.

Best,
Maria

metadata.subject: Data handling for mobile clinic & grant reports

artifacts.content:
Carlos: You still okay to help at St. Mary’s clinic on 4/12?

Maria: Yeah, Mami said she’ll sleep over with the kids Friday so we can be at St. Mary’s Catholic Church by 7:00 a.m.

Carlos: You looked wiped after the last one at the library.

Maria: I was. Remember my BP at Riverbend Urgent Care last May – 168/102? The doc practically ordered me to cut back on the extra 10–12 volunteer hours a week. That’s why I asked Dr. Lewis to adjust my schedule and brought on two co‑chairs.

Carlos: Good. I don’t want another scare.

Maria: Me neither. But this St. Mary’s clinic is important. At the April 2025 one, Paula Jenkins (RCHC diabetes case manager) and I scheduled 11 undocumented parishioners with A1C over 9.0 for after‑hours visits on April 22–23 through the side entrance so they didn’t have to face the main registration desk questions.

Carlos: Wow.

Maria: And don’t forget Roberto at the December 3 library event – your 49‑year‑old custodian friend who skipped BP meds for three months to pay that $600 payday loan. His numbers were through the roof. Or Bob Hanley from our club back in January 2022 at the Riverbend Senior Center, almost failing the vision test because he’d stopped insulin over a $380 balance at Riverbend Pharmacy. We cleared it quietly so he wouldn’t be embarrassed.

Carlos: Okay, okay, I get it. I’ll check the generator and the shelving in the van this weekend.

Maria: Gracias. I’ll handle the volunteer schedule and make sure we have the new LCI consent forms printed so we don’t repeat that late‑2022 data mess.

Planning notes – youth focus & school partnerships

1) Riverbend Middle School
– Sofia’s world. I’ll never forget May 2022 when her 7th‑grade counselor told me two of her friends were being evaluated for learning disabilities while the nurse suspected untreated vision issues. Then Ana called me in tears on May 11 about the $180 eye exam quote from that Main Street optometry office.
– On top of that, October 2022 when Ms. Patel called because Sofia was hiding her school‑issued glasses in math after kids called her “four eyes.” Meeting with Ms. Greene and the pediatrician at Riverbend Family Pediatrics to talk through anxiety and headaches.
– August 3, 2024 Back‑to‑School Sight Day: coordinated with Dr. Melissa Chang at ClearView to waive the $85 exam fee for Sofia and three IEP classmates on free lunch, coding them as “community charity” so it wouldn’t appear on the district billing reports.

2) Lincoln Elementary
– September 2021, Adrian failing the vision screening, headaches, reading delays, “academic concern/possible learning issue” in his file until ClearView diagnosed significant astigmatism. That’s why I push so hard to get every 3rd‑ and 6th‑grader screened, even if the club ends up covering $60–$80 per kid for full exams and glasses.
– November 2024 clinic with Riverbend Unified: nurse Karen Patel quietly flagged six CPS‑involved students for priority screening due to suspected medical neglect. I tracked which four showed up and noted the two families with open CPS cases tied to missed Riverbend Family Eye Care appointments in a color‑coded, password‑protected sheet.

3) To‑dos
– Build a script for volunteers about bullying/teasing around glasses.
– Work with district to formalize a process so vision is checked before LD evaluations.
– Revisit data‑sharing protocols so CPS‑flagged kids are supported without over‑sharing in email.
